data.title: Palantir Is Hiding In Plain Sight: Maintaining The Stock With A Buy
data.description:
I last wrote about Palantir Technologies Inc. (NASDAQ:PLTR) in late February when the stock was trading at $106 per share. The stock is down 12.8% since my article, against the S&P 500 (SP500), down around 11.6% during the same period. My thesis still stands, and I hereon reiterate Palantir with a buy and think every pullback is a gift that keeps on giving. Palantir is a stock I’ve been optimistic about since late September last year when the stock was trading around $36 per share, and it is up around 150% since then, as seen below.

I like the company for the long term, and I continue to advise investors to add on each and every pullback for a lower cost average. I think there’s more upside ahead in the near term after NATO’s operational headquarters committed to using Palantir’s Maven Smart System for military planning within the next 30 days. The acquisition was finalized on March 25, and the contract value wasn’t disclosed. According to NATO Communications and Information Agency General Manager Ludwig Decamps, the new tech will provide

“customized state-of-the-art AI capabilities to the Alliance, and empowering our forces with the tools required on the modern battlefield to operate effectively and decisively.”

I’ve been tracking the Maven Smart System since early November last year after the company signed a five-year contract to expand the system’s AI/ML capabilities across the U.S. military service, including the Army, Air Force, Navy, and U.S. Marine Corps. Quick reminder: customers can give a workload that “required 2,000 staff back during Operation Iraqi Freedom to only require 20 people.” The stock closed up around 5% higher on Monday after the news; Maven started as a classified DoD project but is now becoming the operating system of the West. Palantir has been hiding in plain sight.

Valuation and price movement:

The stock has a beautiful setup, in my opinion, and everything is pointing towards a reversal.

Palantir stock is currently trading at $92.6, above its EMA21 at $86.7, EMA50 at $86.4, and EMA200 at $65. If you look at the chart above, you probably see a few bullish signs right off the bat, with RSI currently in the neutral zone at 50 and the MACD bullish cross, along with these green circles you see above, that I consider a cool bonus. While my thesis is more long-term, I think it’s also important to get in at the lowest price, hence why I also like to predict the price movement based on signs. Fortunately for Palantir bulls, this looks like a good time to add on the pullback as I expect the stock to shoot up from here. These are the levels I’m watching like a hawk:

  1. $86-$88 for a prime add zone where the EMAs are currently forming a cluster.

  2. $91-$92 near-term support level, and $66-$67 for a long-term support level.

  3. $97 is the breakout level for the near term.

  4. $105-$110 as a long-term target zone.

  5. $125 is the all-time high retest zone.

Volume is also spiking up, another bullish sign.

The stock is extremely expensive, according to its valuation metrics, even after the pullback. The stock is trading at 225.9x P/E and 166.9 forward P/E, against the sector median of 19.6x and 18.8x, respectively. EV/Sales ratio is also inflated, with forward EV/Sales at 56.2x, against the sector median at 2.4x, and price/sales and price/book tell the same story.

And if we were to put these metrics aside for a second, the company’s numbers in growth and profitability are a sight for sore eyes. The company’s year-over-year revenue growth of 29% is ahead of the sector median (to say the least) at 5%, with EPS growth at 41%. On profitability, the company’s gross profit margin of 80% exceeds the sector median at 50%, and its net income margin of 16% is also ahead of the sector at 4%.

Investment thesis and what’s next:

I’d argue you get what you pay for: an AI software company that, through its AIP, has no competition yet, making it a first mover in the space, with no alternative product in the market offering Palantir’s AI-governed operational command.

So while the company has competitors in AI and data analytics from Microsoft Corporation (MSFT) and Amazon.com, Inc. (AMZN), Palantir’s AIP integrates data for action-driven decisions uniquely, making it perfect for high-stakes sectors like healthcare and defense. No one has come close, and that’s why you see the company’s edge in government contracts.

My old thesis was three parted, and I’m glad to say last quarter showed momentum picking up. I expect acceleration in both commercial and government revenue to continue into the next quarter. While the company started with government revenue making the big chunk of its business, it slowly but surely diversified into commercial revenue that currently accounts for 45% of its total revenue, up 64% year over year in 4Q24. The company continued its strategy and announced two deals in early March, the first with TWG Global and the second with Société Générale. I expect these to show up in the commercial side of the business over the next 2 quarters.

Additionally, while I was expecting increased new customer conversions and expansions in existing customers through Warp Speed as a long-term tailwind, it’s coming sooner than expected as the company announced six new customers deploying Warp Speed, including Epirus, Red Cat, Saildrone, Saronic, Usra Major, and SNC.

I think Maven is proving closer than ever too, certainly more with the latest NATO news. This doesn’t only concern Palantir, but reflects the border economic and political uncertainty as Europe looks to lessen its dependence on the U.S. for defense.

Palantir’s 1Q25 earnings are coming in early May, on the 5th, to be specific. I expect the company to beat on top and bottom lines and beat higher for 2Q25 as I don’t see headwinds from tariffs and the DeepSeek, from Hangzhou DeepSeek Artificial Intelligence Co., Ltd. (DEEPSEEK) moment on the company. I discussed DeepSeek further in my previous article, so I’m saving you the refresher there.

I’m not optimistic on a lot of stocks like I am on Palantir, and for good reason. Though Palantir has flown under the radar for a while, I think the market is finally awakening to the beast in its midst.

data.title: Nvidia: Already On The Way Back Up After Tariffs Turmoil
data.description:
Just when I thought in my previous article on Nvidia Corporation (NASDAQ:NVDA) in which I highlighted that the going has been tough, I certainly didn't expect the market to take me for another wild ride into the abyss, as both the S&P 500 and the Nasdaq threatened to be engulfed in a gut-wrenching bear market.

While I telegraphed the long-term upside for the stock, NVDA isn’t immune to short-term swings as the market rotated out of growth related plays recently. Fortuitously, we have been spared from a further drubbing (at least for now), as President Trump provided much-needed respite through his now famous/infamous “Blink Wednesday,” as he communicated a 90-day pause on reciprocal tariffs targeting America’s trading partners (aside from China).

In addition, Trump has also extended relief to a broad range of electronic products that would have caused massive pain among US consumers, especially since China is a major exporting country to the US, as the big tech and their partners have developed well-established supply chain in China, that’s arguably nearly impossible to replicate or reshore in the short term. Hence, the back and forth is in full display again for investors, although I’m not about to complain since a further reckoning in the market could have set off greater panic in the market, especially as cracks in the financial system appeared to be forming.

For Nvidia, the respite is very much welcomed, even though a permanent relief remains unlikely. The White House has already triggered an investigation into semiconductors imports, which will likely see the industry being carved out for its own set of tariffs, similar to what the automakers have already been dealing with.

Yet, the silver lining is that even Trump appears to be prepared to remain “flexible” in auto tariffs, as the automakers could see their profits being decimated as the impact in the supply chain starts to percolate. Hence, the threat of them passing on higher costs to consumers is almost a given (the questions now moves on to how much?), which could set off alarm bells about whether higher inflationary expectations could be anchored, complicating the Fed’s responses into lowering interest rates for the rest of the year in view of possibly slowing economic growth.

In other words, I believe the market is likely more assured that while the imposition of semi tariffs is a clear and present danger to the semiconductor supply chain, the president seems prepared for temporary reprieve if the key players could commit to reshoring their manufacturing bases into the US. The White House might deem it imperative to take control of semi manufacturing given its importance to national security, particularly as the US-China trade war is expected to worsen, as the tit-for-tat measures don’t appear to have a clear off-ramp for either party at the moment.

Trump’s punitive tariffs rates on China has compelled Xi Jinping to dig in, while appealing to key trading partners in Southeast Asia to rally around China’s plight, and aiming to extend bilateral cooperation to prevent the US from forming a defensive perimeter through economic coercion, undermining China’s economic interests.

For Nvidia, while its reliance on China revenue has arguably slowed down, CEO Jensen Huang’s appeal to President Trump personally through the Mar-a-Lago diplomacy has yielded positive results, as Trump seems prepared to allow Nvidia to continue selling its H20 AI chips to China’s leading tech companies, in exchange for the Nvidia to commit to a $500B investment in America for domestic manufacturing.

Could these already feature planned expansion opportunities strategically interwoven to fit into Trump’s narrative, like how Apple’s own $500B investment plan appears to be so. Since CapEx discussion and execution takes far more than a few months, it doesn’t seem likely that Nvidia’s announcement is in response to Trump’s reciprocal tariffs, and surely not to the tune of $500B for two weeks’ work. Hence, I believe Jensen has delivered a masterclass rivaling Apple (AAPL) CEO Tim Cook, which even President Trump alluded to in his remarks as he named Cook in comments regarding the recent reprieve provided to electronic products that surely benefited Apple.

However, with the compliance deadline for the AI diffusion rule set to live in May 2025, it could portend another period of apprehension for Nvidia, given its immense exposure to AI data center revenue (almost 90% of its last twelve months total revenue base). The industry has already called out that the wording of the framework in its current form is detrimental to help maintain US long-term leadership in AI, given the marked impact that it could cause onto key allies and partners outside of the US.

I’ve also enunciated just how Alibaba (BABA) appears to be primed to capitalize on any blowback from the rules implementation that could result in less AI computing capacity, hindering the growth cadence of the US hyperscale behemoths. Hence, it isn't without basis that China’s tech giants are prepared to take advantage of a pullback imposed by the rules, possibly hurting Nvidia and the hyperscalers.

It’s already a known fact that China's AI capabilities are making leaps and bounds in their progress since DeepSeek (DEEPSEEK) threw a spanner in the works for America’s leading AI firms earlier this year. So much so that even OpenAI has decided that it’s timely for the company to release open-source models to keep up with the narrative and broaden the adoption in enterprise companies looking to lower their barriers to entry as they experiment and implement AI in their workflow.

Still, the technological marvel of Nvidia’s Blackwell architecture ramp has been progressing well, and TSMC’s (TSM) recent monthly revenue results validates that the structural gains in AI aren’t expected to slow down anytime soon. Deepwater Asset Management also argued that we are still a few years away from a possible AI bubble burst, as Agentic AI takes centerstage in 2025, although we haven’t yet quite see that thesis taking off yet.

Perhaps, business leaders are increasingly worried about the policy flip-flops and abrupt changes in the White House’s policy formulations and implementations that there could be a pause in spending, which might temper the AI enthusiasm (but not cancellation). Yet, given Nvidia’s sensitivity to data center AI spending, it remains to be seen whether Microsoft’s (MSFT) decision to adjust its CapEx footprint might be construed as a broader downturn, although Google (GOOGL) (GOOG) appears to have struck back at such worries with CEO Sundar Pichai committing to its planned CapEx for 2025.

Yet, the question about 2026 could come to the fore in Nvidia’s subsequent management outlook, especially companies are expected to provide less robust or even withhold guidance in view of the economic restructuring that is going on right now. Such ambiguities could morph into decision paralysis if we aren’t careful, and Nvidia’s outlook for 2026 would likely be impacted, although Nvidia's earnings estimates are still well-poised for growth.

In addition, we must also reflect doubts into the execution of semiconductor tariffs, as the extent and implementation timeline remains unknown for now. However, as I mentioned earlier, the opportunity to obtain short-term or even rolling reprieves might be possible, although Trump may demand further spending commitments in exchange for such a breather. How these could affect Nvidia’s outlay and whether its supply chain partners can manage these changes while maintaining profitability remains a big question mark for now.

Despite that, it would be unusual if the market hasn’t reacted to these uncertainties, particularly since they may present substantial obstacles on Nvidia’s AI-driven proposition. And the market is clearly not foolish, as NVDA’s forward EBITDA multiple has dropped to 20.5x, well below its 10Y average of 33.7x. With a forward PEG ratio of 0.66 (more than 50% below its sector median), has the market turned too gloomy on its growth outlook and overplayed Nvidia’s fundamentally strong thesis, as it fends off competitive risks from hyperscalers custom design, while taking on Broadcom (AVGO) as the semiconductor bellwether attempts to disrupt Nvidia’s market dominance.

And we also cannot rule out AMD (AMD) as it seeks to gain its place in the AI value chain, while Intel’s (INTC) takes another stab at its resurgence under CEO Lip-Bu Tan’s leadership. However, I believe the market is justified to feel aggrieved with the geopolitical convolutions engendered by Trump’s widening trade war which could return to haunt us as we get closer to the 90-day deadline on reciprocal tariffs, with little deal resolution with China in the works, while still navigating treacherous terrain on semiconductor sectoral tariffs at the same time.

While buying sentiments on NVDA appears to remain robust last week as the stock seems to have bottomed out, the trend bias could move into a downtrending phase if we cannot hold the $90 decisively, even though NVDA’s valuation is arguably reasonably attractive when adjusted for its potential growth.

Hence, I believe the $75 level might even be in play if the recovery in the market hits a stumbling block, as the market possibly adjusts to a much slower pace of growth for AI enterprise spending, as customers might also reach out to lower priced chips offered by competitors or hyperscalers, although that isn’t my base case for now.

Nvidia’s ability to maintain its growth prospects through incessant innovation as showcased by its recent GTC demonstrates how it has managed to carve out its leadership over its archrivals. Yet, the company’s efforts to amplify the adoption of AI might face a stuttering block as the US government’s initiatives to reorder trade flows and manufacturing exposure could have global ramifications that might send the US economy into a recession, possibly torpedoing NVDA’s valuation multiples.

While these risks are highlighted to remind you to consider them as we take into account its growth prospects, I believe the risk/reward remains primed to the upside, supported by its market dominance, the White House’s support for global AI leadership, NVDA’s battered growth adjusted valuation, and also a possible bottom from last week’s lows.

Rating: Maintain Buy.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: XLU: Safe Haven Assets Could Prove Even More Attractive (Rating Upgrade)
data.description:
Utilities Select Sector SPDR Fund ETF (NYSEARCA:XLU) delivered a strong rally since my previous coverage. The fund has been standing on the green territory since the beginning of the year, while the broad market and the technology sector are in a prominent downfall. Last time I based my Hold rating on the estimated lower upside potential of the utility stocks, which were overshadowed by the technology players. However, the new tariff conditions changed the market situation in favor of the utility sector, which could prove to be an attractive safe haven for investors amid the uncertain macro environment. I believe that XLU has favorable potential in light of the expected energy consumption and capital investment pattern. My estimates give a clue that the fund could deliver up to a 20% total return for the next 12-month period, which makes me bullish on XLU again.

Investment Discussion

XLU offers an exposure to the Utilities Select Sector Index, which includes American utility companies engaged in the supply of electricity, gas, water, and steam.

The fund's industry allocation is focused mainly on electric utilities (64.6% share) and mixed utilities (26.9% share). However, the share of these two core pillars decreased slightly since my previous article in favor of independent power and renewable producers. It is not surprising, providing the growth of worldwide investments in clean energy sources is now almost twice as much as in fossil fuels. In the meantime, the ETF remained quite concentrated around its top 5 positions, which account for 38.4% of the portfolio and are distributed as follows: NextEra Energy (NEE) with 11.9% (vs. 13.7%); Southern Company (SO) with 8.2% (vs. 7.9%); Duke Energy (DUK) with 7.7% (unchanged); Constellation Energy (CEG) with 5.8% (vs. 6.4%); and American Electric Power (AEP) with a 4.7% share (vs. 4.5%).

As of April 9, 2025, the fund manages assets in the amount of $17 billion and charges the investors 0.08% on annum for the management. The AUM value grew by circa $5.4 billion from the date of my last coverage (approximately one year ago) due to the solid performance and a net inflow from investors in the amount of $2.7 billion.

Talking about the performance, last time I wrote that utilities continue to be outshined by the technology sector, which surged considerably thanks to the strong performance of the main chip players. However, from the following chart, we can notice that the state of affairs has changed.

XLU delivered a strong rally of circa 19% for the last 12-month period on the backdrop of a 6% gain in the S&P 500, represented by the SPDR S&P 500 ETF Trust (SPY), and a 3% drop in the broad Technology Select Sector SPDR (XLK). Two main factors for this could be the resilient earning growth of the utilities sector during 2024 and the fresh tariff policies from the new administration. The latter have a great impact on the technology sector due to the US reliance on imports of advanced electronic hardware from the Asian region. Meanwhile, utilities have limited exposure to the tariffs impact on the US economy, which is anticipated to experience a GDP slowdown and an uptick in consumer price inflation. In such an environment, the utilities sector could prove to be well-insulated, as the regulators usually permit passing along higher costs to customers.

Industry Potential

The growth prospects for the industry remain solid, as data centers are a key driver of energy consumption growth in the U.S. (see the chart below) in the coming years. The share of total energy consumption of data centers could reach up to 12% by 2028, which translates to a total power demand of up to 580 TWh. Moreover, the global IT spending in 2025 on data center systems is anticipated to surge by 23.2% YoY, being the fastest-growing segment of the overall IT.

In addition, the industrial sector could also contribute to the energy consumption growth. The U.S. is substantially reliant on advanced chip hardware imports, which, in light of the intensification of the tariff disputes, could only facilitate the commissioning of new semiconductor factories. Thus, the course towards developing domestic production will likely strengthen during the Trump presidency. TSMC already plans to increase U.S. investment in advanced semiconductor manufacturing by $100 billion with three new fabs.

At the same time, rising energy consumption stimulates capital investment expansion by the U.S. utility operators. The CAPEX is expected to accelerate prominently in the next few years, with $212 billion predicted for 2025, which represents a 22% increase from the $174 million spent in 2024. The growth of investments boosts the utilities' rate base, which creates conditions for higher revenue requirements and profit growth.

However, the uncertainty regarding the trade policy creates fears of an economic slowdown in the U.S. The current market sentiment implies four interest rate cuts of 25 bps over the next 12 months. A cut in the key interest rates will have a favorable impact on utility stocks by reducing the interest cost pressure. Moreover, it should lower the government bond yields and make these safe haven assets even more attractive to investors.

Examining the dividend yield of utilities and the monthly yield on 10Y treasuries, the current discrepancy between them seems to be the largest for the last three years. Concerning the dividend yield on XLU, which is 3% on a TTM basis, it could be explained by the solid performance of the utility stocks. On the other hand, the 10Y yield has climbed prominently since the first interest rate cut in September 2024. The main factor for this pattern could be strong economic growth and the slight uptick in inflation during the last year's final quarter. However, with the increased probability of a recession due to the new tariff provisions, the gap between the US10Y and the XLU yield could narrow.

Risk Factors

Natural disasters remain a significant concern, as they can cause damage to the utility infrastructure. The year started with massive fires in California, which led to a rapid drop in Edison International (EIX) shares. An investigation is currently underway into the possible involvement of faulty equipment of the company as a cause of the wildfire. In addition, the regulatory environment is becoming increasingly important for the utility business with the growth of investments in the energy infrastructure. The ability to pass the increased costs on to the end consumer depends on the regulators' flexibility. Concerning the global trade tensions, the negative consequences for the utility business couldn't be ruled out. The electrical transformers are among the list of most imported products in the U.S., with a significant portion of the equipment coming from Mexico and China.

Investment Conclusion

I will stick to the same model for the calculations of the XLU upside potential, which is based on the weighted average target prices of the fund's securities. The upside potential of the XLU should be 16%, which is almost twice that indicated in previous estimates.

We can spot that NextEra Energy, Constellation Energy, and Vista Corp. are deemed to contribute the largest upside potential among the fund holdings, or up to 9% combined. It's not surprising, since NEE benefits prominently from both stable cash flows from its regulated utility and the leadership position in the renewable energy business. CEG stands among the main beneficiaries from the clean energy transition, since the company operates the largest fleet of carbon-free energy facilities. Vistra Corp., in turn, could profit from its integrated business model and flexible energy solutions.

Looking at the earnings estimates, the utility sector is expected to register a 7.7% growth in the first quarter of 2025, which should strengthen the dividend generation capacity of the utility players. According to the DPS forecasts, the forward dividend yield of the ETF could be estimated at 3.4%. Along with the expected 100 bps interest rate cuts over the next 12-month horizon, this could be enough to close the yield gap to 10Y government bonds. As a result, I am upgrading the XLU potential from Hold to Buy, since the utility industry has a solid fundamental to benefit from the growth in energy demand. This could prove the XLU ETF as a tariff-resilient investment, which has the potential to deliver up to 20% total return over the forward 12-month horizon.

data.title: Our Take On The U.S. Tariff Pause
data.description:
Transcript

1) Establishing guardrails

There's a huge amount of policy uncertainty right now. In fact, it has been impossible to predict policy. We find it more rewarding to establish guardrails, to try to understand what could potentially prompt the [U.S.] administration to potentially change the course of policymaking.

It seems to take some account of market volatility, financial risks and pushback from other sources, as well as a country's willingness to engage.

We think that can put a check on its maximal stance, and we also think that eases the risks of a near-term financial accident.

2) Three tariff types

The first type is tariffs on specific sectors to support reshoring of activities. We already had the 25% [tariff] on autos and parts and steel and aluminum. And likely coming up next are semiconductors, pharmaceuticals, copper and lumber. The second type is a bit of a universal 10% to generate revenue for the government that it really needs.

And the third type is country-specific negotiations with countries that have [a] goods surplus with the U.S., and [it’s] likely intended to provide leverage to try to bring down trade imbalance.

3) Extending our tactical horizon

We have been saying that in the near term, because of policy uncertainty, risk assets are likely going to come under pressure and over the longer term, fundamentals should prevail. And [we] were more positive over the six-to-12-month horizon. After April 2, huge amount of policy uncertainty aside, what really worried us was that there didn't seem to be checks on the maximal policy making stance from the U.S. administration, which made us think that we couldn't really see that much further ahead.

And we shortened the technical investment horizon from 6 to 12 months to three months, putting more emphasis on the near-term hunkering down. And at the time we said that we didn't know how long or how short this period would be. Then fast forward to last week, where my biggest takeaway is that there were tracks, after all, on the maximal policy making stance from the U.S. administration.

And yes, of course, looking ahead, there is going to be so much uncertainty, volatility, policy headlines flying around. But the fact that the checks exist gave us confidence to resume the 6–12-month horizon of tactical investing, where we are more positive.

Currently, we’re modestly positive on U.S. equities, especially after the year-to-date drawdown.

And we're modestly positive on Japanese equities on a currency unhedged basis. We are selectively in favor of sectoral opportunities like banks in Europe. We are underweight in [long-term] U.S. treasuries. They are supposed to behave like safe haven assets, and they really haven't been in this new regime of high debt and inflationary pressure. And in fact, gold has been a better diversifier in this new environment for portfolios.

The 90-day pause of tariffs on most countries and exemption of key tech imports suggest the U.S. administration is taking some account of financial risks and costs as well as a country’s willingness to engage. It shows there are factors that could put a check on the administration’s maximal tariff stance. As a result, late last week we extended our tactical horizon back to six to 12 months to dial up risk. Yet we still think tariffs can hurt growth and lift inflation, and major uncertainty remains.

The U.S. has paused country-specific “reciprocal” tariffs on all nations, except China, for 90 days and exempted some key tech imports. These tariffs are intended to create negotiating leverage on countries with which the U.S. runs goods trade deficits – and reduce imbalances. See the chart. Even with the pause, the U.S. average effective tariff rate is still around 20%, including 145% tariffs on select Chinese imports. We see U.S. tariffs adding to inflation. Prolonged uncertainty raises the risk of recession. It may drag on corporate investment and delay longer-term commitments. Consumer spending could be hurt by any erosion of wealth and real incomes. Dented confidence in the U.S. could curb foreign investor appetite for U.S. assets. Trade tensions with China are set to deepen. We see tariffs lowering growth in China, and potential policy stimulus only partly offsetting that drag.

Along with country-specific tariffs, we see two other primary types of U.S. tariffs. First, tariffs on strategic sectors to support reshoring of activity. Second, a universal 10% tariff on most imports to generate revenue and aid domestic production. Even with last week’s pause – and subsequent exemption of some key tech imports such as smartphones – the U.S. is still facing much higher tariffs than we expected a few weeks ago. With uncertainty around where tariffs will land an unpredictable negotiations ahead, we aim to understand the factors that can prompt the administration to change course on policy. It appears to be taking some account of market volatility, financial risks and other sources of pushback, as well as a country’s willingness to engage. That is putting a check on its maximal stance and could bind policy changes.

Checks on policy emerge

The implications? The near-term risk of a financial accident has eased. We cautiously leaned back into risk late last week by extending our tactical horizon back to six to 12 months from three months. We also renewed our overweight to U.S. and Japanese stocks. U.S. equities are supported by the AI theme, resilient corporate earnings and a so far solid economy. We see Japanese stocks still benefiting from stronger corporate profits and shareholder-friendly reforms. We recently upped Europe’s stocks to neutral but focus on selective opportunities while looking for more progress on structural challenges.

Yet, we expect ongoing risk asset volatility and potentially sharp reversals. Spiking yields in long-term U.S. Treasuries seemed to be a factor in the change in tariff tactics. We stay underweight long-term Treasuries, our highest conviction view: tariffs are likely to add to already sticky inflation, and congressional budget plans last week reinforce the outlook for persistent budget deficits. We favor gold instead as a portfolio diversifier. The broad-based equity selloff has created opportunities to tap into certain sectors, and selectivity is key. We still like U.S. technology benefitting from the AI buildout and adoption. We also favor global banks. That includes U.S. banks given the scope for deregulation even with some potential economic pain. We also like banks in Europe (higher rates versus pre-pandemic levels) and Japan (stronger loan growth).

Our bottom line

The U.S. paused most “reciprocal” tariffs even as U.S.-China trade tensions look set to deepen. Checks on policy allowed us to extend our tactical horizon back to six to 12 months and resume our positive view on U.S. and Japanese stocks.

This post originally appeared on BlackRock

data.title: Miller Deep Value Select Strategy Q1 2025 Letter
data.description:
Rising Uncertainty Leads to Greater Long-Term Opportunity

2025 has been a bit of a roller coaster. The year started strongly, with the market making new highs in mid-February, followed by a double-digit drawdown into quarter end. Sentiment quickly turned lower on rising economic uncertainty as the administration moved towards rolling out global tariffs. Marketplace fears are rising, discounting slower future economic growth and a potential second wave of inflationary pressures caused by tariff implementation. Corporate leaders have also highlighted the recent rise in uncertainty. Nearly half of the S&P 1500 companies mentioned tariffs during their recent earnings calls, up 233% from the prior quarter.

Shortly after the fifth anniversary of the global Covid outbreak, the U.S. Economic Policy Uncertainty Index1 quickly approached 2020 peak levels (the only two occurrences above 400 over the past 40 years). Current policy uncertainty levels are more than 50% higher than the U.S. debt downgrade during August of 2011. As we have seen in the past, equity prices, which discount future expectations, dislike rapidly rising levels of uncertainty. It is no surprise that investors have sought out “safe haven” money market funds, which are at new record levels at greater than $7T and up more than 16% over the past year.

US policy uncertainty index

Price volatility is often a welcome occurrence to long-term oriented value investors. Volatility is not risk; permanent capital loss is the main risk for investors. With equity prices representing ownership pieces of an underlying business, a sharp fall in market prices can create very attractive long-term investment opportunity. The greater the market price discount to the business’s long-term fundamental value, the more attractive the investment opportunity.

While we view the recent drawdown as an attractive opportunity, we still see risk in the longest duration equities which fueled periods of marketplace speculation over the past couple of years. History has also shown that market corrections help to remove these market excesses. The most expensive subset still made up a significant portion of the market and have embedded market expectations for significant growth into the future along with very high valuation multiples. During the quarter, the total market cap of stocks with price-to-sales greater than 20x reached an all-time high $6T! That’s $1T higher than 2022’s peak and is nearly 2x the Dot Com peak! As Kailash Capital recently highlighted, one has had two times better odds at a casino than having success investing in stocks with valuation multiples over 20x sales over the long term.

History has shown that the most expensive portion of the market has consistently lagged the overall market returns over the long term. Is there a lesson from the 2000 and 2022 time periods? Overlooked low valuation stocks had market leading returns coming out of these peak speculation time periods. From 2000 to 2006, Small-Cap Value and Mid-Cap Value led the market, significantly outperforming Large Cap Growth by 150-215%!

As we highlighted in our recent quarterly letters, lower valuation securities continue to have lower representation in passive Indexes; they are under-owned and under-followed by the marketplace. Greater volatility and wider valuation spreads further enhances the potential for attractive future investment returns. There are an increasing number of companies in the market today, trading at a single-digit price-to-earnings multiple. Today, the lowest valuation subset is close to a 20% earnings yield, which is five times higher than the 10-year bond and nearly four times higher than the overall market.

The uncertain environment has also caused the marketplace to increase its probability of a recession taking place during 2025. While it's possible that lower business and consumer confidence leads to a recession, we would expect a shallow and short one being more likely rather than a deep and prolonged recession. In fact, the current drawdown for small cap cyclicals from November 25th highs is greater than the average drawdown seen during the first six months of a recession. The black bars in the chart highlight the average recession drawdown in the first 6 months, looking back 100 years.

Miller Deep Value Select Strategy Composite Returns

Past Performance is no guarantee of future returns. The Miller Deep Value Select strategy incepted 10/1/2018. Performance periods greater than one year are annualized. The “gross” of fees performance figures reflect the day-weighted performance of assets (including cash reserves) managed for the period and do not reflect the deduction of our investment management fees or performance fees but do reflect the deduction of trading commissions and withholding taxes. Throughout this document, the “net” of fees performance figures reflect the deduction of trading commissions; withholding taxes; the reinvestment of dividends, interest and other cash flows; a model performance fee; and a model investment management fee equal to the maximums under our fee schedule for Deep Value Select separate accounts. The following is our fee schedule for new Deep Value Select strategy separate accounts (as reflected above): the model performance fee is 20% of the account’s performance, after the investment management fee has been deducted, above a 6% hurdle rate; the model investment management fee is 1.00% on all assets under management. These model fees have been deducted from the gross returns on a monthly pro-rated basis to arrive at the net of fee performance shown above. Historical separate account investors may have paid lower fees than the model fees used to calculate “net” of fees performance here. The model performance fee is calculated and accrued on a monthly basis and paid out annually. For important performance information, please reference the Deep Value Select GIPS Composite Disclosure.

During the quarter, the Deep Value Select strategy had a -12.8% drawdown, lagging the S&P 1500 Value Index -.2% and the S&P 600 Value Index -9.9%. After reaching an intra-quarter peak, the strategy had a greater than -15% drawdown. Looking back, since the strategy inception, a 15%+ drawdown was a rare occurrence. The seven other occurrences lasted one to four months and all saw a positive performance recovery, looking out to 6 months and 1 year. Average 6-month returns from the trough were +36.16% and 1-year average returns were +90.77%.

During the quarter, the strategy’s largest positive contributor was Gray Media (GTN), with shares up +37%. Gray significantly lagged the strategy during the back half of 2024. While the company’s political advertising led the overall market, it fell below market expectations. Gray continues to generate strong free cash flow and successfully paid down more than $500M of debt during the past year. While the company has significant debt leverage, Gray has a much smaller amount of debt maturities over the next two years. Marketplace expectations for Gray’s future retransmission revenues remain very low, providing a nice ongoing variant as management focuses on improving long-term retransmission agreements. Near-term risks include an advertising recession; auto advertising may be weaker over the coming months; however, local advertising has so far remained resilient. Gray has the potential to deliver strong free cashflow next year and $2B+ over the coming 5 to 6 years. Ongoing debt reduction should accrue to the equity over time. We see long-term upside potential multiples of the current share price.

Our two largest detractors during the quarter were Gannett (GCI) and Nabors Industries (NBR), with shares down 43% and 26%, respectively, during the quarter. Both companies’ share prices are at deep discounts to our view of their long-term fundamental value, and we have recently increased position sizes in both holdings.

Gannett has had ongoing success with its long-term transformation to a digital media company. Digital revenues (subscriptions, advertising, media services, partnerships, and e-commerce) are expected to become half of Gannett’s revenue later this year as the company focuses on monetizing its 200M monthly audience reach. The secular headwinds of the traditional newspaper business remain as the most obvious near-term risk. While this portion of the business may continue to decline, Gannett has ongoing cost reduction opportunities to drive greater operating efficiencies. Gannett shares remain significantly mispriced in our opinion at only .17x revenue and >50% normalized free cash flow yield. New York Times (NYT), which went through a similar digital transformation ten years ago, has a much higher valuation level, with price to sales at 3x and Enterprise Value to EBITDA (“EV/EBITDA”) greater than 15x. In addition, Gannett’s anti-trust suit versus Google is very similar to the DOJ anti-trust suit against the company. Kellogg Hansen, a firm that specializes in anti-trust cases, with successful historical awards and appeals of more than $1B, is representing Gannett. The law firm agreed to success-based compensation for their services. Winning the anti-trust case would accelerate Gannett’s transformation and accrue significant value to shareholders. We believe the current share price reflects limited value for the long-term prospects of the transformation plan and the significant chance of prevailing in the anti-trust case.

Nabors recently completed the acquisition of Parker Drilling. The transaction is accretive to free cash flow per share. Parker provides Nabors greater access to the growing lateral drilling marketplace. In addition, the transaction will significantly increase Nabors Drilling Solution (“NDS”) segment, which has market-leading technology and strong global growth opportunities. With very low capital intensity and high free cash flow conversion, Nabors NDS segment should support accelerating future free cash flow generation. While there is ongoing near-term risk on weaker than expected domestic and international drilling expenditures, we see these risks significantly discounted in the current share price. While there is also potential integration risk with doing an acquisition, the Parker Drilling transaction provides significant margin of safety in our opinion (attractive cost savings, balance sheet benefits, potential non-core asset sales and accelerating free cash flow generation). Nabors share price looks extremely attractive, below one times cash flow (approaching their Covid 2020 all-time lows). At a greater than 50% normalized free cash flow yield and EV/EBITDA (2025) below 3x, we see long-term upside potential multiples of the current share price.

During the quarter, we made a new investment in an attractively priced building supplier. JELD-WEN (JELD) is a leading North American and European manufacturer and distributor of interior and exterior doors and windows to new construction and remodeling sectors. JELD-WEN share price has been under significant pressure, 73% below its 52-week high as the company has experienced revenue and profit weakness. The company is undertaking a multi-year transformation, bringing in automation and system enhancements to enhance their manufacturing and warehouse footprint. Since 2023, new senior management has removed $350M from their cost structure and management expects $100M/year in further productivity savings. As they rationalize their infrastructure over the next five years there is significant capital efficiency improvement potential. In addition, like our investments in Quad Graphics (QUAD) and United Natural Foods (UNFI), JELD-WEN owns real estate, which provides a significant margin of safety for the transformation plan. While the company expects margins and profits at historical trough levels in the early part of 2025, a new cost reduction program should support margin and profit improvement later in the year. Near-term risk is a weaker housing and remodeling marketplace, causing greater near-term revenue weakness and a slower recovery in company margins. Long-term positive supply and demand dynamics (favorable demographics and limited supply) should shorten the current downturn. In addition, there was a recent market transaction that bolsters our view JELD-WEN’s significant embedded value proposition. Owens Corning acquired Masonite International (interior/exterior doors) in May 2024 at 8.6x EV/EBITDA. JELD-WEN should be a beneficiary of any housing recovery, with 25-30% incremental margins on a future volume improvement. Long-term upside potential from their transformation plan supports the goal of achieving double digit normalized EBITDA margins and could realistically support a share price multiples the current price level.

While Deep Value Select experiences monthly volatility of returns, these are not capital impairments as there have only been a small handful of occurrences where we have sold an investment for a loss. We have recycled capital from mostly successful historical investments into new investments or holdings that have lagged the strategy historical returns. Strategy portfolio measures today are approaching historical extreme levels. On average, the strategy’s holdings ended the quarter 42% below their recent 52-week high, which is close to the historical average performance trough for Strategy holdings. Deep Value Select valuation measurers are also approaching historical lows, on average at less than 1.5x cash flow, price to sales less than .2x and normalized earnings and free cash flow greater than 30%. We thank our clients for their patience during these drawdown time periods. We see significant return potential in the strategy holdings today and remain steadfast in our investment approach, focused on delivering attractive long-term returns.

Dan Lysik, CFA

1The US Policy Uncertainty Index measures policy-related economic uncertainty using three underlying components: news coverage, federal tax code provisions, and the Federal Reserve’s Survey of Professional Forescasters. For more information, please visit policyuncertainty.com.

References to specific securities are for illustrative purposes only. Portfolio composition is shown as of a point in time and is subject to change without notice.

Contributors and Detractors mentioned above are those holdings that had the greatest effect on the Strategy’s composite performance for the quarter. For information on how Contributor/Detractor data are calculated and their effect on composite performance, contact us. For more information about Miller Value management fees, please contact us.

Past performance cannot predict future results. It is not possible to invest directly in an index. Unmanaged index returns do not reflect any fees, expenses, or sales charges.

The Russell 1000® Growth Index measures the performance of those Russell 1000 Index companies with higher price-to-book ratios and higher forecasted growth values. The Russell 1000® Value Index measures the performance of those Russell 1000 Index companies with lower price-to-book ratios and lower forecasted growth values. The Russell 2000® Value Index measures the performance of the small cap value segment of the US equity universe. The Russell 2000® Growth Index measures the performance of the small cap growth segment of the US equity universe. The Russell Midcap® Value Index measures the performance of the mid- cap value segment of the US equity universe. The Russell Midcap® Growth Index measures the performance of the mid- cap growth segment of the US equity universe. Batting average is a statistical method that measures an investment manager’s ability to meet or beat the returns of a benchmark index. Price-to-Sales (P/S) is a valuation metric that compares a company’s market capitalization to its total revenue, indicating how much investors are willing to pay for each dollar of sales. CRSP US Large Cap Index includes U.S. companies that comprise the top 85% of investable market capitalization. CRSP US Mid Cap Index targets U.S. companies that fall between the top 70% – 85% of investable market capitalization. CRSP US Small Cap Index targets inclusion of the US companies that fall between the top 85% and 98% of investable equity market capitalization. The S&P 1500 Index includes stocks in the S&P 500, S&P 400, and S&P 600 and covers approximately 90% of the market capitalization of U.S. stocks. Price to earnings is the market price per share divided by earnings per share. Earnings Yield is a valuation metric that refers to the earnings per share for the most recent 12-month period divided by the current price per share. The S&P 1500 Value Index draws constituents from the S&P 1500 Index. Value is measured on three factors: the ratios of book value, earnings, and sales to price. The S&P 600 SmallCap Value Index tracks the value stocks in the S&P 600 SmallCap Index, identified by three factors: book value, earnings and sales to price. An investor cannot invest directly in an index. Free cash flow is earnings before depreciation, amortization, and non-cash charges minus maintenance capital expenditures. Free cash flow yield is an overall return evaluation ratio of a stock, which standardizes the free cash flow per share a company is expected to earn against its market price per share. The ratio is calculated by taking the free cash flow per share divided by the share price. Enterprise Value to Earnings Before Income, Taxes, Depreciation, and Amortization (EV/EBITDA) is the enterprise multiple and is used to determine the value of a company. EBITDA is earnings before interest, taxes, depreciation and amortization and is a calculation of a company’s financial health.

Any views expressed are subject to change at any time, and Miller Value Partners disclaims any responsibility to update such views. The information presented should not be considered a recommendation to purchase or sell any security and should not be relied upon as investment advice. It should not be assumed that any purchase or sale decisions will be profitable or will equal the performance of any security mentioned. Past performance is no guarantee of future results, and there is no guarantee dividends will be paid or continued.

©2025 Miller Value Partners, LLC
